topic: Decision Making to Improve Marketing Performance
subject: business-studies

items.q: Is the product with the lowest price always the most competitive?
items.correct: No — competitiveness depends on the whole mix: quality, brand, convenience and service. Low prices can even signal poor quality and erode profit margins.
items.misconception: Cutting price is always the best way to increase sales and market share.
items.random_wrong: Businesses are legally required to match competitors' prices.
items.why: Students equate cheap with competitive, ignoring non-price competition and the role of price elasticity.

items.q: Does correlation in marketing data prove one variable causes the other?
items.correct: No — correlation shows an association only; a third factor or coincidence may explain it, so marketers should test before acting on it.
items.misconception: If advertising spending and sales rise together, the advertising must have caused the sales rise.
items.random_wrong: Correlation can only be calculated for prices, not sales.
items.why: Students leap from correlation to causation, a classic error when interpreting marketing data.

items.q: Is market research a guarantee of a successful product launch?
items.correct: No — research reduces risk, but samples can be unrepresentative, consumers may not act as they say, and markets change between research and launch.
items.misconception: Thorough market research removes the risk of product failure.
items.random_wrong: Market research is only legal for products aimed at adults.
items.why: Students overstate what research can deliver, forgetting sampling error and changing consumer tastes.

items.correct: The firm's sales divided by total market sales, times 100
items.misconception: Total costs divided by total revenue, times 100
items.random_wrong: This year's sales divided by last year's sales
items.why: Correct answer: The firm's sales divided by total market sales, times 100. “Total costs divided by total revenue, times 100” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: If price rises 10% and quantity demanded falls 25%, price elasticity of demand is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: -2.5, meaning demand is price elastic. “-0.4, meaning demand is price inelastic” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which is an example of secondary market research?
items.random_wrong: Conducting a face-to-face survey of shoppers
items.why: Correct answer: Analysing published government census data. “Running a customer focus group” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Forecasting future sales by extending the trend in past data is called:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Extrapolation. “Segmentation” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A firm's sales are £2 million in a market worth £25 million. What is its market share in %?
items.why: The real figure is 8 %. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
